sentences.text: It's actually a very, very important part of my whole relationship with-- with Miss Richman, with Abram with Andy.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Andy, as a transport officer, always helped Abram with the transport.
Miss Richman instructed Andy to provide transport for children whose parents were in Poland, and whose parents requested that the children go back to-- to-- to them in Poland.
And my instructions from Miss Richman was that she had received from the Red Cross a list of names of children whose parents were in Poland and who were resident in Rosenheim.
I had to collect the children and instruct them and the madrichim that the following day, we would transport back-- transport them back to the borders.
And at the border, the Red Cross would take the children back to the parents and their homes in Poland.
And as a social worker, it was very important for me to reunite family because that's-- that was one of the aspects that I was trained.
One of the most important part of social work is to reunite families and to make them as stable and as well adjusted as possible.
Where children are separated from their parents, one expects problems.

And so with the instructions from Miss Richman, I got hold of all the children and told all the madrichim that they must be ready the following morning, that they were going to be taken back to their parents.
I actually didn't speak to the children.
I spoke only to the madrichim who would accompany them to the border.
And everything was arranged.
Andy had the trucks all ready.
Miss Richman had arranged for food for the trucks.
And we had a party that night.
The UNRRA had a party to celebrate the fact that the children were going back.
And the next morning, when I went to the camps and to the madrichim, there were no children and no madrichim.
And I couldn't understand it.
There were other children in the-- in the dormitories and other madrichim.
And I said, where are so-and-so, and so-and-so, and so-and-so?
And they said, well, we were brought here with trucks.
And so I said to Andy, Andy, what's happened to the children that were here?
He said, I don't know.
Speak to Abram.
And I confronted Abram in an absolute fury.
I said, what have you done with the children that were in this dormitory, in that dormitory, in the next dormitory?
And he said, Lily, they're at another camp.
I said, why?
You knew that they were going back to their families.
And he looked at me and he said, Lily, you are so naive.
We want those children and their families to come to Palestine.
If we send them to another camp, the parents would follow.
And we will at some stage go back for the parents and reunite them with the children here, and send them on to Palestine.
If you send the children back to Poland, that'll be the end of them as Jews and as-- as communities that would come to Palestine.
There's no ways that they would come to Palestine because the Poles won't let them out.
And I just couldn't understand it.
I was so angry, so frustrated, so ashamed to go back to Miss Richman and say, I can't find any of the children.
And he said, Lily, in 20 years' time, when you see me, you'll realize that I've done the right thing.
Of course, I couldn't believe that.
And I was-- I just lost my temper and-- and went out, and really sheepishly went to Miss Richman and said, I can't find any of the children.
Abram has transported them out of here.
And she laughed.
She said, well, I suppose he knows what he's doing.
And then I went to Andy, and I said, Andy, what have you done?
He said, Abram wanted trucks.
I gave him trucks.
I don't ask him what he does with the trucks as long as the trucks are back where they should be in the morning, and they're here.
And that was the end of the children being reunited with their family.
But as a-- just to complete that whole picture, years, years later, when I met Abram in Israel, and he-- he said-- and we laughed.
He took me and he said, well, what do you think, all those children that were reunited with their families?
So I said, what children?
They weren't reunited.
And he said, well, as a matter of fact, those children were on the exodus, and are now in-- in Israel.
And their families, most of them followed them to Israel.
So this is what the Jewish Agency wanted-- to have as many families in Palestine and out of Europe, which is not-- not the place for Jews to be in.
So that's the story of the transport.
And I think I was the only one who was-- was angry.
I don't think Miss Richman was angry.
I don't think Andy was angry.
And of course, Abram thought he was a great guy, which he was.
Do you have any criticisms of the DP camps?
In retrospect, no.
But at the time, I had the criticism of the black market, and that they weren't clean.
They weren't-- they weren't as hygienic as the-- as the Americans are.
They weren't anal in their-- [LAUGHS] in their habits of cleanliness.
But I think it's-- in retrospect, it's very difficult to-- for one madrich to be able to really keep the place in-- in-- in perfect condition.
But I think I should mention this now about the cupboards in the-- in the dormitories and in-- and in-- with the families.
I think at the beginning of '47, the Americans were rather interested in what had happened in the camps.
And a group of American congressmen were visiting the displaced persons camp.

And I got word from Miss Richman that I must go around through to all the dormitories and to clean-- clean them up, to make sure that the place was clean, because Americans would only go back and-- and say, we can take these people as immigrants on condition things were very good.
There was a very, very low-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not-- a very low ratio, I think it is, or-- of Polish immigrants who could go to America.
The best ratio was the German.
So a lot of Poles got German papers and could go to Poland quite-- to America quite easily.
When it came to the Polish immigrants, it was rather difficult.

And the American congressmen were coming out to have a look at the camps-- the Lithuanian camp-- the camps with the Lithuanians and the Polish survivors or the Polish immigrants were-- Polish people who wanted to immigrate were.
And so one of the duties that I had was to make sure that everything was spick and span and clean.
And I went through to the dormitories.
And I was horrified at the amount of stale bread that we found in the cupboard.
And also, the cupboards were dirty.
They weren't clean.
And when I think about it, bread was the-- was the survival kit that a lot of these people had.
If they had bread, they could survive.
And when they left the dining halls in the-- in the displaced persons camp, they always took a few slices of bread with them just in case they wouldn't be able to get bread again.
I think it was really-- it wasn't necessary.
They would get bread again.
But I think that their experience with such that one always needed that extra piece of bread.
And I was disgusted that-- that the cupboards were dirty.
And what would the American senators say if they looked in the cupboards and they saw how dirty everything was?
But behind it all, of course, was the Jewish Agency.
They didn't want the camps to look clean, and hygienic, and-- and well laid out, because the Americans would then go back and say, yes, we must take these people out of sympathy.
And-- and so they would send in a very, very positive report.
And of course, then more immigrants would get to America than to Palestine.
And the Jewish Agency sabotaged our plan of cleanliness.
And instead of making the place look clean and-- and very beautiful for the American congressmen, they made it look shabby, and dirty, and-- and unfavorable.
And in fact, they even brought in animals from the German countryside to show what sort of people these Poles were, that they were ready to live with animals.
And again, of course, behind it all was Abram and the Jewish Agency.
And when the American congressmen came, and Miss Richman took them through to the dormitories, she was as disgusted as I was.
Of course, I was in the-- Miss Richman, myself, and Abram, and the congressmen were taken through to the-- to the dormitories.
And it was absolute bedlam.
It was ugly.
The-- the-- the-- the beds weren't made.
The cushions were on the floor.
The cupboards were dirty.
There was dust all over.
And I realized, again, to my horror and frustration, that the Jewish Agency were behind it.
And we didn't make-- I couldn't make any excuses.
Miss Richman I think made some excuses-- excuses to the American congressmen.
And in their politeness, they thanked us and left.
But Miss Richman, actually, at that stage was very angry with-- with Abram because she said that she had put her-- he had put her in disgrace, that she wasn't able to keep the place clean.
And he explained the reason.
And I think she understood.
But she nevertheless felt that she-- she was actually not assisted by Abram, but she was sabotaged by him and the other Jewish Agency people.
But I think in retrospect, again, what he did was in order to get as many immigrants to Palestine, and not to the outside world, to the rest of the world.
I couldn't-- I couldn't accept it.
But Miss Richman and Andy were more mature than me, and certainly Abram was.
And that was accepted by them.
I think those sort of clashes that I had with the Jewish Agency, because I really wasn't as honest at that stage.
I was more interested in my professionalism and my moral attitudes.
And I think that's due-- that was due to my immaturity, not to my maturity.

And so it was difficult for me to realize that there were black marketeers in the world, and there were people who wanted Palestine to be Israel, and that I wasn't cooperating with them the way I should have.

And I think also the-- the relations, my-- my uncle and his black marketeer-- black market activities were not something that I accepted very lightly-- again, through immaturity, not through any idealism.

I think the most rewarding experience for me was the fact that I was able to establish nursery schools, that I was able to give the children an education, the fact that the people at the camp felt that I was helping them, and the fact that when they left, they left in gratitude, not in anger.
In February '47.
Yes, yes.
I went to work in the Municipal Social-- Department of Municipal Social Work.
And then when I got married, I gave up social work and worked in nursery schools.
The Southern-- Southern [INAUDIBLE] Nursery School, and then Oxford Nursery School.
In Johannesburg, yes.
Strangely enough, no.
The impact came very much later.
My immediate return-- was to get married, and to have a family, and to talk about my work in Germany, because there were very few people who knew about it.
And I gave quite a lot of public talks about work in the displaced persons camps, but not with the kind of impact that it had when I was much older, when I actually wrote the manuscript that I did.
Then I relived it much more so than I did at first.
I was too busy working at nursery schools and bringing up a family to really be bothered about what-- what I had gone through.
Well, I hadn't gone through anything.
The-- the experience that I had.
I was in contact with Gertrude Starnever, who returned to London.
And then I heard from her in America.
As a nursery school principal.
And much later, I decided to do a higher degree in education.
And when I graduated with a B.Ed, I was offered a job to work with retarded children.
And I remained with-- working with retarded children until I retired last-- a month ago, two months ago.
Jacob Lipman.
Now?
My eldest son, Jeffrey, is 47.
He's professor of medicine at Wits University.
And my youngest son, Frank Lipman, is a-- a doctor in New York, but he practices alternative medicine.
None at all.
None at all.
Frankie's wife perhaps a little more, because she went to a Jewish day school and has much more Jewish interests than Jeffrey, who is only interested in his research work.
Many years after.
My children were almost certainly adolescents-- not while they were growing up.
I wrote that in I think '81, which is 30-- nearly 40 years later.
It was called And--
I think I started becoming interested in Holocaust.
It was quite an important subject that was being brought up over and over again in Europe and in South Africa.
And I think that when I look back, it was a very important part of my becoming very Jewish.
When I left South Africa for Germany, I had very little interest in Jewish affairs.
But I think in the late '70s and early '80s, I suddenly realized that the Jews have a tremendous history and a tremendous culture, and I know nothing about it.

So in my usual, very intensive way, I started reading about Jewish history and Jewish culture, and realized that anti-semitism is something that one should be very much aware of, and that the fact that I worked in the camps was a result of anti-semite-- anti-semitic views that were held in Europe pre-war, and probably centuries old.
And I became very, very Jewish-orientated-- not religious, but very Jewish-orientated.
And I started collecting all the literature and all the books that were written about the Holocaust.
And now I have an enormous library of Holocaust literature.
I think that my first-- the first thing that I think struck me was there but for the grace of God could I have been.
If we left in 1929, it was really just far-- '29, '39-- about 12 years before the onset of the war, or 10 years, really. 10 years, because the war started in '39.
It was 10 years before the onset of the war.
And I really escaped by virtue of the fact that my parents-- my father, particularly-- had the foresight to leave Europe.
And in a way, I felt, I suppose, guilty that I had survived when so many of my family did not survive.
My father's family were all wiped out, except for one cousin in Warsaw that I visit regularly.
My mother's family survived in greater numbers, although most of them were in concentration camps or in orphanages during the war.
And when I re-met them in Europe, as I did, I think it must have had some kind of impact.
They are survivors and these are their experiences.
And you didn't really identify with them when you were in-- in Germany.
You felt almost alienated to them.
And then suddenly, that alienation just disappeared.
And now I have-- I feel almost part of them, one of them.
My uncle Zally, the children-- his children-- my cousin Garrick, who's at Harvard, and his sister, who's recently died, who were brought up and brought into America through Uncle Zally.
And--
Well, Garrick was in Stutthof with Uncle Zally, who was in Stutthof.

The-- Zally's children were taken out of the Kovno ghetto and put into orphanages-- three different orphanages with four-- three different children by Zally's sister-in-law, who looked non-Jewish because she was blonde and she spoke German fluently.

She took the-- she stole the children one evening and, with the help of some Lithuanian Christian children, put them into three different orphanages and only collected them after the war, when-- She survived by living as a Christian.

And after the war, she collected the three children and brought them-- took them to Berlin, and then heard that Zally, who'd got out of Stutthof, was in Munich, contacted him, and told him that his children were alive, but his wife had-- had not survived the ghetto.
She was killed in the ghetto.
And he went to fetch them, his two children, and the other child that-- whose parents were killed in the war.
The other child was Ellen, who's a sister of Garrick.
And Garrick had joined the Hashomer and was in the camp.
In-- in fact, he was in-- in Rosenheim.
And he left with the underground for Palestine, one of the illegal-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] one of the illegal transports through to Haifa.
Hour In fact, he didn't reach Haifa.
He landed up in Cyprus, but eventually went to Haifa.
And he went to university.
And he had-- he went into camp at the age of six and got out at the age of 10, so he virtually did his whole schooling in about four years.
And because of his very, very high IQ, he got a bursary to go to America.
And he studied at the Los Angeles school, and then at Chicago.
And he's now a professor at Harvard in agricultural economics.
Grilrches, G-R-I-L-R-C-H-E-S.
And his sister remained on with Uncle Zally and the two children, and came to America with him.
Z-I-E-V.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
That one must not lose the Jewish identity.
That the Jews have survived because of their Jewishness.
That one doesn't survive as a Jew if you forget the Jewish culture and what the Jewish people have lived through all these centuries.
And I think the Jews are superior.
It's a dreadful, racist remark to make.
But wherever the Jews have gone, they have done extremely well.
Most of the survivors that I have been in touch with in-- in America and in Israel have done extremely well.
I suppose I've still got a bit of the ghetto mentality, but to me, education perhaps has the most important [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] thing in-- the most important thing is to be educated.
And I was I was a-- a very bad mother because I was more interested in my sons doing well at school than well at sports.
My sons, on the other hand, think it's more important for their children to be good at sports rather than to be good at-- at school.
It's good to be good at school, but you've got to be a sportsman.
And that's not really a Jewish phenomenon.
I think the Jewish phenomenon is to-- to burden your children with education rather than anything else.
And I think I've got that from my experience in Germany.
I think that to me, the Jewish experience was an education experience.
And I'm very grateful that my parents pushed me into studying.
And I think that I've done right by pushing my children into a good academic career, both of them.
